mnemonics.title: Special Variables Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: P-S-?-$: $$=Process ID, $?=Exit Status, $*=All Args Single, $@=All Args Separate, $#=Count, $0=Name, $1-9=Arguments, $!=Background PID, $$=Current PID

mnemonics.title: Test Operators
mnemonics.content: File Tests: -f=file, -d=dir, -r/w/x=permissions, -e=exists, -s=size>0. String: -z=empty, -n=non-empty, = equal, != not-equal. Numeric: -eq, -ne, -gt, -lt, -ge, -le.

acronyms.usage: Most common Linux shell, supersedes Bourne shell (sh)

acronyms.expansion: Simple Hardware Embedded Language and Logic
acronyms.usage: Acronym for the command interpreter in Unix/Linux systems
Connects commands; output of one becomes input of another
Defines field delimiters for word splitting, default is space, tab, newline

visualTips.visualization: Imagine keyboard -> stdin (file desc 0), screen -> stdout (file desc 1), error -> stderr (file desc 2). Redirection changes these flows using < > 2> >> operators.
Write script -> Save (.sh) -> Make executable (chmod +x) -> Execute (./script.sh or bash script.sh) -> Shell reads line by line -> Commands execute -> Exit status returned.
For loop: Initialize -> Check condition -> Execute body -> Update -> Repeat. While: Check condition -> Execute body -> Repeat. Until: Execute body -> Check condition -> Repeat.

analogies.analogy: Like a waiter in a restaurant takes your order (commands), communicates to kitchen (OS), and brings back food (output). The shell takes your commands, sends to kernel, returns results.
Like labeled boxes in a warehouse. Variable name is the label on box, value is what's stored inside. You can look up (echo $box) or change contents anytime.
Like an assembly line in a factory. Raw material (input) goes through multiple stations (commands), each transforming it, producing final product (output).
